name: NYC Local Law 144 (Automated Employment Decision Tools)
description: New York City law requiring an independent bias audit, public disclosure of audit results, and candidate notice for automated employment decision tools (AEDTs) used to substantially assist or replace discretionary decisions in hiring or promotion for New York City-based candidates or employees.
verification_note: Verified via web search 2026-07-09 (Week 2); re-verified 2026-07-10 (Week 3 parallel cleanup) against the official NYC Administrative Code library (codelibrary.amlegal.com) via search-indexed content -- direct fetch of that page returned HTTP 403, so this relies on the search engine's indexed snippet of the primary source's own section titles/text, not a full direct read. That snippet confirms the citations below (section 20-870 = Definitions, including 'bias audit' and 'employment decision'; section 20-871 = the operative prohibition on using an AEDT without a bias audit, public summary, and notice) -- this corrects Week 2's citation mapping, which had the two sections' roles reversed. Local Law 144 was enacted 2021-12-11 and took effect 2023-01-01, with active enforcement beginning 2023-07-05 -- these are two distinct dates and this dataset deliberately uses the earlier one: effective_from=2023-01-01 represents when the law's obligations legally began binding covered employers, not when DCWP enforcement activity started; the 2023-07-05 enforcement date is recorded here as context only. As of 2026, a December 2025 New York State Comptroller audit found weak DCWP oversight, prompting a stricter 2026 enforcement phase (more frequent investigations, higher cumulative penalties) -- an enforcement-posture change, not a substantive-requirement change. Core requirements (bias audit within one year of use, public posting of audit summary/date/distribution date, candidate/employee notice) and the bias-audit content (testing for disparate impact by sex, ethnicity, and race per federal EEO component-1 categories; auditor independence) are confirmed by multiple sources including the primary code library. NEEDS_OWNER_REVIEW (unresolved): the precise statutory definition of 'substantially assist or replace discretionary decision-making' and the exact carve-outs/exemptions are still synthesized from secondary compliance-guidance sources, not a full read of the primary Admin Code text or the DCWP final rule (both blocked or too large to fetch directly this session) -- verify the full text before this dataset backs a client deliverable. Sources: NYC Administrative Code § 20-870 (Definitions) and § 20-871 (amlegal.com code library, https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/newyorkcity/latest/NYCadmin/0-0-0-135839); NYC DCWP, Automated Employment Decision Tools (https://www.nyc.gov/site/dca/about/automated-employment-decision-tools.page); Deloitte, 'NYC Local Law 144-21 and Algorithmic Bias' (https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/services/audit-assurance/articles/nyc-local-law-144-algorithmic-bias.html); NY State Comptroller, 'Enforcement of Local Law 144' (2025-12-02) (https://www.osc.ny.gov/state-agencies/audits/2025/12/02/enforcement-local-law-144-automated-employment-decision-tools).

applicable_aedt.name: Automated Employment Decision Tool Subject to LL144

criteria.summary: A computational process (including one derived from AI, machine learning, statistical modeling, or similar techniques) used to substantially assist or replace discretionary decision-making in hiring or promotion, for a candidate or employee who would work in New York City.
examples: Resume screening or ranking tools that score or rank NYC-based candidates
Automated interview scoring or video-analysis tools used in NYC hiring
Candidate-matching or job-recommendation tools that substantially assist a NYC hiring or promotion decision

citations: NYC Admin. Code § 20-871 (prohibition on use without bias audit, public summary, and notice)

criteria.summary: The tool is not used to substantially assist or replace a discretionary hiring or promotion decision, or the candidate/employee/position has no New York City nexus.
exemptions: Tools used solely for scheduling or logistics, with no scoring or ranking role in the decision
Positions with no New York City nexus
